definition: Describing conditioned phenomena as unstable, changing, and not lasting.
notes: This entry is treated as a major doctrinal term because it functions within the three marks framework and in close relation to dissatisfaction and not-self. The default project preference is the adjective impermanent in direct translation, while impermanence may be used in explanatory prose when the abstract quality itself is being discussed.

context_rules.context: In direct doctrinal statements describing phenomena
context_rules.notes: Default project rendering for the adjective.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose naming the characteristic itself
context_rules.notes: Use when English needs the abstract noun rather than the adjective.

context_rules.context: In passages emphasizing instability or unreliable changeability
context_rules.notes: Acceptable alternate when impermanent sounds too static.

example_phrases.notes: Classic three-marks formulation using the adjectival sense.
Shows the doctrinal linkage from impermanence to dissatisfaction.
